name: Zonules.com Claim Registry
note: Every scientific claim is supported by a registered source, marked as conceptual interpretation, marked as internal framework, or excluded.

claims.statement: The zonules of Zinn are fine fibrous strands that suspend the crystalline lens from the ciliary body.
The zonular apparatus, also called the suspensory ligament of the lens, holds the lens in centered geometric tension.
Tension transmitted through the zonules allows the ciliary muscle to change lens shape during accommodation, shifting focus across distances.
The structure is named after Johann Gottfried Zinn, the 18th-century anatomist who described it.
The zonules are the literal instance of the suspension layer (FIO-01): focus is produced by hidden structural tension, not by the lens alone.
The ciliary body is a ring of tissue behind the iris that contains the ciliary muscle and gives rise to the zonular fibers.
Contraction of the ciliary muscle reduces tension on the zonules, allowing the elastic lens to become more convex for near focus.
The ciliary body also produces aqueous humor, the fluid that fills the anterior segment of the eye.
The ciliary body is the force source of the suspension layer (FIO-01): it does not focus light but governs the tension that makes focus adjustable.
The crystalline lens is a transparent, biconvex structure suspended behind the iris that refracts light to help form an image on the retina.
The lens is elastic and changes curvature during accommodation.
The lens is enclosed in an elastic capsule into which the zonular fibers insert.
The lens is the focal element of the suspension system: present but unable to focus reliably without zonular tension.
Accommodation is the process by which the eye changes its optical power to maintain a clear image of objects at varying distances.
During accommodation for near vision, the ciliary muscle contracts, zonular tension decreases, and the lens becomes more spherical.
Accommodative ability typically declines with age, a condition known as presbyopia.
Accommodation is the operational expression of FIS-2: focus integrity maintained across an intended operating range.
The lens capsule is a transparent, elastic basement-membrane envelope that surrounds the crystalline lens.
The zonular fibers insert into the lens capsule around the lens equator, transmitting their tension to the lens through the capsule.
The elasticity of the capsule helps mold the lens into a more curved shape when zonular tension is released during accommodation.
The lens capsule is the transmission interface of the suspension layer: the surface through which hidden tension becomes a change in focal geometry.
Visual perception is the process by which the brain organizes and interprets visual input into a stable, meaningful representation of the environment.
Perception is constructive: the visual system actively organizes input rather than passively recording it.
Selective attention determines which parts of the visual field are processed in detail.
Perception is the Layer 02 instance of the suspension thesis: attention holds a focal interpretation in place the way the zonules hold the lens.
Machine vision is the field of methods by which artificial systems detect, classify, localize, and interpret content in images and video.
Machine vision systems can produce correct detections while failing to interpret a scene correctly.
Establishing the origin and authenticity of an image is addressed by content provenance standards rather than by detection alone.
Machine vision is the Layer 03 instance of the focus-integrity thesis: calibration and grounding play the suspension role.
Selective attention is the mechanism by which the visual system prioritizes some stimuli for detailed processing while suppressing others.
Attention can be directed voluntarily (top-down) or captured involuntarily (bottom-up) by salient stimuli.
Attentional capacity is limited, so not all of the visual field can be processed in detail at the same time.
Selective attention is the perceptual suspension mechanism (FIO-01): it holds a focal target in place.
Figure-ground organization is the perceptual process of segregating a scene into objects (figure) and background (ground).
Figure-ground assignment can be ambiguous, as demonstrated by reversible figures.
Gestalt principles describe how the visual system groups elements into coherent figures.
Figure-ground separation is the perceptual instance of signal-noise integrity (FIO-03).
Deepfakes are synthetic media in which a person's likeness is generated or manipulated using machine-learning methods.
Detection methods analyze artifacts, inconsistencies, or statistical signatures to classify media as authentic or synthetic.
Detection alone does not establish origin; content provenance approaches record an image's source and edit history.
Deepfake detection is a focus-integrity problem of provenance (FIO-04).
Image provenance is the recorded history of where an image came from and how it was modified.
Content provenance standards attach tamper-evident metadata to media to support authenticity verification.
Provenance complements detection by establishing origin rather than inferring manipulation from the pixels alone.
Image provenance is the infrastructure of provenance integrity (FIO-04).
The cornea is the transparent, avascular anterior surface of the eye and provides approximately two-thirds of the eye's total refractive power.
Corneal transparency depends on the precise arrangement and hydration of collagen fibrils in the stroma.
The cornea is a focus-integrity element of signal quality (FIO-03): its transparency determines whether the optical signal is clear before it reaches the lens.
The retina is the light-sensitive tissue lining the posterior interior of the eye, containing photoreceptors and multiple layers of neural processing cells.
The retina converts incident light into neural signals through phototransduction.
The retina is the anatomical site where focus integrity is decided (FIO-05).

Photoreceptors are specialized retinal cells that transduce light into neural signals; rods are sensitive to low light and motion, cones are concentrated at the fovea and detect color and fine detail.
The highest density of cone photoreceptors is at the fovea.
Photoreceptor density and distribution determine the spatial resolution and luminance sensitivity available to the visual system.
Photoreceptors are the signal-noise boundary of anatomy (FIO-03).
The optic nerve is the bundle of approximately one million axons that carry retinal ganglion cell output from the eye to the lateral geniculate nucleus and primary visual cortex.
Damage to the optic nerve causes permanent loss of the visual field it carries.
The optic nerve is the structural conduit of vision (FIO-01): without its physical integrity, no signal reaches interpretation.
Perceptual constancy is the tendency to maintain a stable representation of an object despite variations in viewing angle, distance, illumination, or context.
Classical forms include size constancy, shape constancy, and lightness constancy.
Constancy depends on the visual system's ability to discount conditions of observation and infer invariant properties.
Perceptual constancy is the perceptual analogue of interpretation integrity (FIS-5).
The Gestalt principles describe how the visual system organizes elements into perceptual wholes; proximity, similarity, continuity, closure, and figure-ground are the core grouping laws.
Gestalt organization reflects fundamental tendencies of the perceptual system to structure visual input into coherent units.
Failure of Gestalt organization produces an unstable or fragmented percept.
Gestalt principles are the structural grammar of perceptual signal separation (FIO-03).
Computer vision is the field concerned with enabling machines to extract meaningful information from digital images and video.
Modern computer vision systems use learned representations, with deep convolutional networks having become dominant for most recognition tasks.
Computer vision systems operate on pixel arrays; input signal quality directly affects downstream task performance.
Computer vision is the machine layer in which all five FIO failure classes can be instantiated.
Image classification is the task of assigning a categorical label to an image from a predefined set of classes.
Classification performance depends on the model's ability to learn discriminative features that generalize.
Distribution shift is a systematic source of classification failure even in high-accuracy systems.
Image classification is a focus-integrity problem of interpretation (FIO-05).
The iris is the pigmented muscular diaphragm that controls the size of the pupil.
The iris contains sphincter pupillae and dilator pupillae muscles allowing pupil constriction and dilation.
The iris is the accommodation layer of the eye's signal input (FIO-02).
The vitreous humor is the transparent, gel-like substance filling the posterior chamber, accounting for approximately two-thirds of the eye's total volume.
The vitreous humor provides the spatial separation between lens and retina required for the optical system to focus.
The vitreous humor is a structural element of focus integrity (FIO-01): it maintains the geometry of the optical system.
The macula is the central zone of the retina, approximately 5 mm in diameter, containing the highest concentration of cone photoreceptors.
The fovea at the center of the macula is the region of sharpest fixation.
The macula is the anatomical site of signal precision (FIO-03).
Depth perception is the ability to perceive relative distance from two-dimensional retinal images, achieved through binocular and monocular cues.
Binocular disparity is the primary cue for depth at close range; stereopsis is the neural process of resolving this disparity.
Monocular depth cues including perspective, familiar size, occlusion, and motion parallax allow depth inference from a single eye.
Depth perception is an interpretation problem (FIO-05): the system must construct a 3D representation from 2D signals.
Inattentional blindness is the failure to notice a fully visible but unexpected stimulus when attention is directed elsewhere.
Inattentional blindness demonstrates that conscious visual awareness requires attention as well as sensory input.
The probability of inattentional blindness increases when the unexpected stimulus is dissimilar to the attended target.
Inattentional blindness is a provenance failure (FIO-04): the input has no provenance in working memory.
Visual working memory temporarily stores and actively maintains visual information; its capacity is limited to approximately three to four objects.
Visual working memory enables comparison of a current scene with a recently stored representation.
The limited capacity of visual working memory creates predictable failures of comparison and change detection.
Visual working memory is the accommodation system of perception (FIO-02).
Object detection identifies and localizes object instances in an image, producing a category label and spatial boundary.
Object detection systems must generalize across scale, orientation, lighting, and occlusion variations.
False positives and false negatives are the symmetric failure modes of detection, controlled by a confidence threshold.
Object detection is an interpretation task (FIO-05): it requires correct categorization and correct localization.
Adversarial examples are inputs that have been imperceptibly perturbed to cause incorrect model outputs.
High accuracy on standard benchmarks does not guarantee robustness: a model can fail on inputs that appear normal to a human observer.
Adversarial robustness is a distinct property from standard accuracy and must be specifically engineered.
Adversarial examples are signal-noise failures (FIO-03): imperceptible perturbations defeat the signal-noise separation the model relies on.
The aqueous humor is the clear fluid filling the anterior and posterior chambers, supplying nutrients to the avascular cornea and lens.
Aqueous humor is continuously produced by the ciliary body and drained through the trabecular meshwork.
The aqueous humor is a structural maintenance element of focus integrity (FIO-01).
The primary visual cortex (V1) receives the principal visual input from the lateral geniculate nucleus and performs the first cortical stage of visual processing.
Neurons in V1 are tuned to edge orientation, spatial frequency, and direction of motion, decomposing the retinal image into elementary features.
The visual cortex is where interpretation begins (FIO-05): cortical failure produces a defect of interpretation rather than of sensing.

The pupillary light reflex is the automatic constriction of the pupil in response to increased light, mediated by a reflex arc involving the retina, optic nerve, pretectal nucleus, and iris sphincter.
The pupillary light reflex is consensual: light directed into one eye produces constriction in both pupils.
The pupillary light reflex is an automatic accommodation mechanism (FIO-02).
Sustained attention is the capacity to maintain focused attention on a task over an extended period.
Vigilance performance typically declines over time on monotonous monitoring tasks — the vigilance decrement.
Sustained attention is distinct from selective attention: selective attention concerns which stimulus is prioritized, sustained attention concerns maintaining that prioritization across time.
Sustained attention is the temporal dimension of perceptual suspension (FIO-01): the vigilance decrement is suspension failure across duration.
Change blindness is the failure to detect a substantial change when it coincides with a brief visual disruption.
Change blindness demonstrates that the visual system does not maintain a complete detailed scene representation.
Changes to currently attended objects are detected readily; changes to unattended objects frequently go unnoticed.
Change blindness is a provenance failure across time (FIO-04).
Visual saliency is the property by which some scene elements stand out and attract attention automatically, driven by local feature contrasts.
Bottom-up saliency operates rapidly and involuntarily, prioritizing locations before task-driven control is applied.
Computational saliency models predict where observers look by computing feature contrast maps.
Visual saliency is the signal-prioritization stage of perceptual signal separation (FIO-03).
Geometric calibration establishes the relationship between a camera's image coordinates and the 3D world.
Accurate calibration is a prerequisite for tasks depending on spatial measurement; calibration error propagates into every downstream geometric estimate.
Calibration can drift over time due to thermal, mechanical, or optical changes.
Calibration is the suspension system of machine vision (FIO-01): it holds the model's spatial reference frame in place.
Domain shift occurs when deployment input distribution differs from training distribution, degrading performance.
Common sources include changes in lighting, sensor characteristics, geographic context, and object appearance.
Domain adaptation and domain generalization address performance across distribution shift.
Domain shift is the accommodation failure of machine vision (FIO-02).
The fovea centralis is a shallow depression at the centre of the macula, approximately 1.5 mm in diameter, containing the highest concentration of cone photoreceptors in the eye.
At the foveal centre, overlying retinal layers are displaced laterally, leaving only cone photoreceptors to receive the focused image with minimal optical scatter.
The fovea contains no rod photoreceptors; it is entirely cone-mediated, conferring high-acuity, colour-sensitive vision in the central visual field.
The fovea is the anatomical zero-point of focus integrity (FIO-03): every optical element exists to deliver a clean signal to this location.
The optic chiasm is the X-shaped junction at the base of the brain where nasal retinal fibres from each optic nerve cross to the contralateral hemisphere; temporal fibres remain ipsilateral.
Compression of the optic chiasm produces bitemporal hemianopia, loss of both temporal visual fields, because the crossing nasal fibres are selectively damaged.
The optic chiasm is a suspension element of the visual pathway (FIO-01): it maintains the structural routing necessary for binocular signal integration.

Each optic tract carries post-chiasmatic visual fibres from the ipsilateral temporal retina and the contralateral nasal retina, representing the contralateral visual field, from the optic chiasm to the lateral geniculate nucleus.
The retinotopic organisation of the visual field is preserved through the optic tract: neighbouring fibres carry signals from neighbouring points in the visual field.
The optic tract is a suspension element of the visual pathway (FIO-01): a non-redundant structural conduit whose failure collapses signal delivery to the cortex.

The lateral geniculate nucleus is a layered thalamic relay that receives visual input from the optic tract and projects to the primary visual cortex, maintaining retinotopic organisation and segregating signals into magnocellular and parvocellular streams.
The LGN receives substantially more input from cortical feedback than from the retina, making it an active gating station that modulates signals based on attentional state and arousal.
The lateral geniculate nucleus is a suspension element of the visual pathway (FIO-01): it maintains the active signal channel between the retina and the visual cortex.
Binocular vision integrates input from two laterally separated eyes into a single percept; binocular disparity is the primary cue for stereoscopic depth at close range.
Binocular fusion requires the oculomotor system to maintain alignment of both eyes on the same point; misalignment produces diplopia or suppression of one eye's input.
Binocular rivalry results in perceptual alternation between incompatible images from the two eyes, demonstrating active perceptual selection.
Binocular vision is an interpretation problem (FIO-05): the system must construct a depth representation from the disparity between two signals.
The oculomotor system controls eye movement through six extraocular muscles per eye, producing saccades, smooth pursuit, vergence, and the vestibulo-ocular reflex to direct and stabilise gaze.
Vergence eye movements work together with lens accommodation and the pupillary light reflex as the accommodative near triad.
Saccadic suppression reduces visual sensitivity during saccades; the perceived stability of the visual world is a perceptual construction from fixation-period snapshots.
The oculomotor system defines the accommodation range of gaze (FIO-02): it sets the envelope of positions across which high-acuity fixation can be directed.

The sclera is the tough, fibrous outer coat of the eye forming approximately five-sixths of the globe's outer surface, maintaining the eye's spherical shape and providing extraocular muscle insertion points.
At the optic nerve head the sclera forms the lamina cribrosa through which retinal ganglion cell axons exit; elevated intraocular pressure damages this structure in glaucoma.
The sclera is a suspension element of the eye's optical system (FIO-01): it maintains the geometric shell within which the optical components are held in their correct spatial relationships.

The choroid is the highly vascular layer between the sclera and retina, supplying oxygen and nutrients to the outer retinal layers including the photoreceptors and retinal pigment epithelium, which are avascular.
The choriocapillaris beneath the macula has the highest blood flow per unit tissue of any structure in the body, reflecting the exceptional metabolic demands of macular photoreceptors.
The choroid is a metabolic suspension element of focus integrity (FIO-01): it sustains the living infrastructure of phototransduction.
Pattern recognition is a cognitive process by which the visual system identifies stimuli by matching sensory input against stored memory templates or prototypes.
Gestalt principles — proximity, similarity, continuity, closure — describe the brain's automatic tendencies to group visual elements into coherent patterns.
Pattern recognition operates at multiple processing levels simultaneously, including low-level feature detection and high-level categorical recognition.
In the Zonules FIO framework, pattern recognition is the primary site of FIO-05 Interpretation failures, where prior expectations distort the reading of incoming signal.
The visual system routinely fills in incomplete information, inferring object identity, surface continuity, and depth from partial cues.
Visual inference is governed by prior probabilities — the brain combines current sensory evidence with learned expectations to construct a most-likely percept.
Predictive coding models propose that the brain continuously generates predictions about incoming stimuli and only propagates prediction errors, not raw sensory data.
In the Zonules FIO framework, visual inference is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation because it introduces the risk that expectations override fidelity to the actual optical signal.
Perceptual load theory proposes that high-load tasks exhaust limited attentional capacity, suppressing processing of task-irrelevant stimuli automatically.
Under low perceptual load, spare cognitive capacity is allocated to distractors, increasing susceptibility to irrelevant stimulation.
Perceptual load interacts with selective attention, with high load producing more focused, effortless exclusion of competing visual information than low load conditions.
In the Zonules FIO framework, perceptual load maps to FIO-02 Accommodation: it defines the effective aperture of attentional focus available to any given visual scene.
Top-down processing uses stored knowledge, contextual expectations, and prior experience to guide interpretation of incoming visual signals, often overriding raw sensory evidence.
Context effects in visual perception — such as reading ambiguous letters differently depending on surrounding words — demonstrate the power of top-down priors.
Top-down and bottom-up processing interact continuously; neither operates in isolation in natural visual scenes, with each modulating the other across processing hierarchies.

In the Zonules FIO framework, top-down processing is the mechanism most directly implicated in FIO-05 Interpretation failures, as prior knowledge can systematically distort reading of an accurate signal.
Attention capture is the involuntary reorientation of visual attention driven by stimulus salience — features such as abrupt onset, high contrast, or sudden motion automatically recruit attention.
The distinction between goal-directed (endogenous) and stimulus-driven (exogenous) attention determines whether attentional shifts are voluntary or reflexively triggered by environmental events.
Salient distractors can capture attention even when observers are explicitly instructed to ignore them, demonstrating the automaticity of certain capture mechanisms.

In the Zonules FIO framework, attention capture is classified under FIO-03 Signal-Noise Separation: a salient but irrelevant stimulus generates noise that competes with the target signal for attentional resources.
Perceptual errors arise from systematic biases in sensory processing, context effects, prior expectations, and limits of neural computation, and are distinct from sensory noise.
Optical illusions are canonical demonstrations of perceptual error, where the visual system's heuristics for interpreting depth, size, and brightness produce demonstrably incorrect interpretations.
Perceptual errors are not random failures but often the predictable output of adaptively useful heuristics operating outside their domain of validity.
In the Zonules FIO framework, perceptual error is the observable output of FIO-05 Interpretation failure: the point where a well-transmitted signal is assigned an incorrect meaning.
Visual ambiguity occurs when a retinal image is consistent with more than one physical scene configuration, requiring the visual system to resolve it using priors or additional cues.
Reversible figures — such as the Necker cube or Rubin vase — are canonical demonstrations of perceptual ambiguity, where the same image alternates between two stable interpretations.

The visual system typically resolves ambiguity by selecting the most probable interpretation given learned priors, but this resolution can be unstable when competing interpretations are equally probable.

In the Zonules FIO framework, signal ambiguity is classified under FIO-03 Signal-Noise Separation: it represents the condition where signal definition is insufficient to support a stable, confident read.
Visual scene parsing involves simultaneous segmentation of figure from ground, grouping of elements into objects, and assignment of spatial relationships between objects.
Gist perception — the rapid, holistic extraction of a scene's category and dominant properties within the first 100 ms of viewing — precedes detailed object-level parsing.
Scene parsing is influenced by both bottom-up salience (contrast, motion, novelty) and top-down knowledge (scene schemas, expected object locations).

In the Zonules FIO framework, visual scene parsing is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: the act of assigning meaning and structure to a visual scene introduces interpretation-layer risk even when the optical signal is clean.
Optical flow describes the apparent motion of image brightness patterns across a sequence of frames, computed from frame-to-frame differences in pixel intensity.
Dense optical flow methods estimate a motion vector for every pixel in the image; sparse methods track selected feature points across frames.

Optical flow is subject to the aperture problem: local motion is inherently ambiguous without global context, because a moving edge seen through a small aperture is consistent with multiple motion directions.

In the Zonules FIO framework, optical flow is classified under FIO-01 Suspension: temporal coherence in motion estimation depends on maintaining a structural correspondence between frames that the aperture problem can undermine.
Scene understanding requires integrated operation of object detection, semantic segmentation, depth estimation, and relational reasoning to produce a globally consistent scene interpretation.
Scene understanding systems trained on standard benchmarks struggle to produce globally consistent interpretations under distribution shift, novel object categories, or unusual viewpoints.
Scene understanding performance degrades systematically under occlusion, rare object categories, and viewpoints not represented in training data.

In the Zonules FIO framework, scene understanding is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: it represents the highest-level integration task in machine vision, where failure to correctly interpret a scene persists even when individual components detect correctly.
Synthetic media encompasses outputs of generative systems including GAN-generated images, diffusion model outputs, NeRF renderings, and text-to-image synthesis.
The indistinguishability of synthetic media from authentic capture constitutes a provenance integrity failure: origin can no longer be inferred from appearance alone.
The C2PA standard provides cryptographically anchored metadata recording origin, edit history, and tool provenance, enabling authenticity verification independent of pixel analysis.

In the Zonules FIO framework, synthetic media is classified under FIO-04 Provenance: the structural challenge is that appearance alone cannot establish origin, requiring an independent provenance chain.
Visual grounding requires identifying the image region referred to by a natural-language expression, linking linguistic semantics to spatial location in the image.
Referring expression comprehension is the canonical visual grounding task, requiring a model to localize a unique referent described by a natural-language phrase.

Visual grounding benchmarks reveal gaps in compositional understanding: models can succeed on individual words while failing on multi-attribute expressions requiring joint spatial and categorical reasoning.

In the Zonules FIO framework, visual grounding is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: it requires the system to produce a correct interpretation of how language and image jointly specify a region.
Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel in an image, partitioning the image into regions corresponding to recognized categories.
Instance segmentation extends semantic segmentation by distinguishing individual instances of the same class, assigning each pixel both a category label and an instance identity.
Semantic segmentation fails silently on out-of-vocabulary regions: pixels belonging to unrecognized categories are assigned to the nearest known class rather than flagged as unknown.

In the Zonules FIO framework, semantic segmentation is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: assigning meaning to every pixel is an act of interpretation that can fail systematically without signalling failure.
Ophthalmic AI systems have demonstrated diagnostic performance comparable to specialist clinicians on specific grading tasks such as diabetic retinopathy screening from fundus photographs.
Ophthalmic AI performance is sensitive to acquisition device, image quality, patient demographics, and disease prevalence, with performance varying substantially across deployment contexts.
Regulatory clearance for ophthalmic AI requires demonstration of safety and effectiveness across diverse populations and acquisition conditions beyond benchmark performance.
In the Zonules FIO framework, ophthalmic AI is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: the system's task is to interpret retinal images, and interpretation failures carry direct clinical consequence.
Medical imaging AI has achieved expert-level benchmark performance across several modalities including radiology, pathology, and ophthalmology, on specific task-benchmark combinations.

Performance gaps between benchmark conditions and clinical deployment have been documented across medical imaging AI, driven by distribution shift, patient population differences, and workflow integration factors.
Medical imaging AI outputs require integration with clinical context for appropriate use; autonomous AI decision-making in diagnosis is not supported by most current regulatory frameworks.

In the Zonules FIO framework, medical imaging AI is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: translating image data into clinical meaning is the interpretation task, and the gap between benchmark and deployment is the interpretation failure mode.
Neural network interpretability methods include feature visualization, saliency mapping, activation analysis, probing classifiers, and mechanistic circuit analysis.
Attribution methods such as Grad-CAM generate pixel-level explanations of model decisions by computing gradients with respect to feature map activations.
High-accuracy models can rely on spurious correlations in training data, achieving correct benchmark performance for incorrect reasons that fail under distribution shift.

In the Zonules FIO framework, neural network interpretability is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: interpretability methods exist because the model's internal interpretation of its input cannot be assumed correct even when its output is.
Retinal ganglion cells are the output neurons of the retina whose axons form the optic nerve and carry visual signals to the brain.
They receive processed input from bipolar and amacrine cells and encode features such as contrast and motion before transmission.
Distinct ganglion cell populations, including magnocellular and parvocellular pathways, convey different aspects of the visual signal.
Retinal ganglion cells are the interpretation gateway of the eye (FIO-05): the final retinal encoding stage that determines what reaches the brain.
The blind spot, or optic disc, is the region of the retina where the optic nerve exits and which contains no photoreceptors, producing a gap in the visual field.
Because the optic disc lacks photoreceptors, no visual signal originates from that location.
The visual system normally fills in the blind spot so that the gap is not consciously perceived.
The blind spot is a provenance gap (FIO-04): a region where the perceived image is constructed rather than recorded, with no underlying signal of origin.
Saccades are rapid ballistic eye movements that shift the fovea from one point of interest to another.
During a saccade visual sensitivity is briefly reduced, a phenomenon called saccadic suppression.
Saccades reposition the high-acuity fovea because detailed vision is limited to a small central region of the retina.
Saccades are an accommodation mechanism of gaze (FIO-02): they repeatedly re-aim the eye to hold focus on changing targets.
Smooth pursuit is a voluntary eye movement that tracks a moving target to keep its image stable on the fovea.
Unlike saccades, smooth pursuit continuously matches eye velocity to target velocity.
Smooth pursuit generally requires a moving stimulus and cannot be sustained over a stationary scene.
Smooth pursuit is a continuous accommodation of gaze (FIO-02): focus held on a target across its motion.
The extraocular muscles are the six muscles that rotate each eye within the orbit to direct gaze.
They comprise four rectus and two oblique muscles, each producing characteristic eye rotations.
Coordinated action of the extraocular muscles of both eyes maintains binocular alignment.
The extraocular muscles are the actuators of the accommodation-of-gaze system (FIO-02): the mechanism that physically re-aims focus.
The optic radiations are the white-matter fiber tracts carrying visual signals from the lateral geniculate nucleus to the primary visual cortex.
Fibers serving different parts of the visual field follow distinct trajectories, including the temporal detour known as Meyer's loop.
Damage to the optic radiations produces characteristic visual field deficits that localize the lesion.
The optic radiations are a suspension pathway (FIO-01): structure that holds the signal's spatial organization en route to cortex.
The retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) is a single layer of pigmented cells between the photoreceptors and the choroid that supports photoreceptor function.
The RPE recycles visual pigment, phagocytoses shed photoreceptor outer segments, and absorbs stray light.
RPE dysfunction is implicated in retinal degenerations such as age-related macular degeneration.
The RPE is a maintenance-suspension layer (FIO-01): hidden support that keeps the photoreceptors operating.
The conjunctiva is the thin transparent mucous membrane covering the sclera and lining the inner eyelids.
It contributes to the tear film and protects the ocular surface from debris and infection.
Inflammation of the conjunctiva, called conjunctivitis, is a common ocular surface condition.
The conjunctiva is a signal-noise boundary (FIO-03): it keeps the ocular surface clear so the optical path stays uncorrupted.
The trabecular meshwork is the spongy tissue at the iridocorneal angle through which aqueous humor drains from the eye.
It regulates aqueous outflow and thereby helps maintain intraocular pressure.
Impaired outflow through the trabecular meshwork is a major mechanism of open-angle glaucoma.
The trabecular meshwork is a pressure-suspension element (FIO-01): hidden regulation that keeps the globe's geometry stable.
Ocular fixation is the maintenance of gaze on a single location, holding the target's image on the fovea.
Even during fixation the eyes make tiny involuntary movements: microsaccades, drift, and tremor.
These fixational eye movements prevent the perceptual fading of a stabilized image.
Fixation is the ocular suspension of focus (FIO-01): active stabilization that holds the focal target in place.
The tear film is the thin fluid layer coating the ocular surface that maintains a smooth optical surface on the cornea.
It has lipid, aqueous, and mucin components that together lubricate, nourish, and protect the surface.
An unstable or deficient tear film degrades the corneal optical surface and visual quality, as in dry eye.
The tear film is a signal-noise layer (FIO-03): the first optical surface, kept clear so light enters uncorrupted.
Contrast sensitivity is the visual system's ability to distinguish luminance differences between adjacent regions.
It varies with spatial frequency, a relationship described by the contrast sensitivity function.
Contrast sensitivity can be reduced even when fine-detail acuity is preserved.
Contrast sensitivity is a signal-noise capacity (FIO-03): the threshold at which a figure separates from its background.
Visual acuity is the capacity to resolve fine spatial detail, typically measured as the smallest discriminable feature.
Acuity is highest at the fovea and falls rapidly with eccentricity into the periphery.
Acuity depends jointly on optical quality, photoreceptor sampling density, and neural processing.
Visual acuity is a signal-fidelity measure (FIO-03): the upper bound on the detail the system can faithfully resolve.
Motion perception is the process by which the visual system detects and interprets movement in the visual field.
Specialized mechanisms respond selectively to the direction and speed of moving stimuli.
Motion can be perceived even from static or intermittent stimuli under certain conditions.
Motion perception is an interpretation process (FIO-05): movement is inferred from sampled signals rather than read off directly.
Color perception is the visual system's construction of color from the spectral composition of light.
Trichromatic cone signals are processed into opponent color channels.
Perceived color depends on context and surrounding illumination, an effect known as color constancy.
Color perception is an interpretation (FIO-05): color is assigned by the system, not a property read directly from light.
Visual masking is the reduction or elimination of the visibility of one stimulus by another presented in close spatial or temporal proximity.
Masking can occur when the mask precedes the target (forward masking) or follows it (backward masking).
Masking is widely used to study the time course of visual processing.
Visual masking is a signal-noise failure (FIO-03): a present signal is suppressed by competing input.
Visual search is the task of locating a target among distractors in a visual scene.
Search can be efficient and parallel or inefficient and serial, depending on target-distractor similarity.
Feature-based search differs in difficulty from conjunction search, where targets are defined by combinations of features.
Visual search is a suspension of attention (FIO-01): focus is held and moved deliberately until the target is found.
Apparent motion is the perception of movement produced by stationary stimuli presented in rapid succession.
It underlies the perception of continuous motion in film and electronic displays.
The phenomenon depends on the spatial and temporal separation between the successive stimuli.
Apparent motion is an interpretation (FIO-05): motion is constructed by the visual system where none physically occurs.
The binding problem concerns how the visual system combines separately processed features, such as color, shape, and motion, into unified object representations.
Attention is thought to play a role in correctly binding features to objects.
Failures of binding can produce illusory conjunctions, where features are mis-combined.
The binding problem is an interpretation challenge (FIO-05): coherent meaning must be assembled from distributed signals.
Bistable perception occurs when a single unchanging stimulus alternates between two stable interpretations.
Reversible figures and other ambiguous images are classic examples of bistable perception.
The competing interpretations alternate over time without any change in the stimulus itself.
Bistable perception is a signal-noise ambiguity (FIO-03): the same signal supports competing readings.
Perceptual priming is the facilitation of recognizing a stimulus due to prior exposure to a related stimulus.
Priming can occur without conscious awareness of the prior exposure.
It reflects how prior experience shapes subsequent perception.
Perceptual priming is an interpretation bias (FIO-05): prior input tilts how the current signal is read.
Visual illusions are systematic discrepancies between the perceived and physical properties of a stimulus.
They reveal the constructive assumptions the visual system makes about the world.
Many illusions persist even when the viewer knows the true nature of the stimulus.
Visual illusions are interpretation errors (FIO-05): the system's inferences override the available signal.
Vision-language models are machine learning systems that jointly process images and text to perform tasks such as captioning and visual question answering.
They learn shared representations that link visual and linguistic information.
Their outputs can be fluent yet ungrounded in the actual content of the image.
Vision-language models are an interpretation layer (FIO-05): meaning is assigned by linking modalities, where grounding can fail.
Pose estimation is the machine vision task of inferring the spatial configuration of objects or human bodies from images.
Human pose estimation localizes body keypoints such as joints to recover posture.
Accuracy degrades under occlusion and unusual viewpoints.
Pose estimation is an interpretation (FIO-05): spatial structure is inferred from appearance rather than measured directly.
Depth estimation is the machine vision task of inferring distance to scene points from images.
It can use stereo disparity, motion parallax, or monocular cues learned from data.
Monocular depth estimation is inherently ambiguous and relies on learned priors.
Depth estimation is the machine instance of depth perception (FIO-05): three-dimensional structure inferred from two-dimensional input.
Visual SLAM (simultaneous localization and mapping) is the technique by which a system builds a map of an environment while tracking its own position using camera input.
It estimates camera motion and scene structure jointly over time.
Positional drift accumulates without loop closure or an external reference.
Visual SLAM is a suspension mechanism (FIO-01): it holds a stable spatial frame as the camera moves through the world.
Feature extraction is the process of transforming raw image data into representations that capture informative structure for downstream tasks.
Classical methods use hand-designed descriptors, while modern methods learn features directly from data.
The quality of extracted features bounds the performance of downstream recognition.
Feature extraction is a signal-noise step (FIO-03): it separates informative signal from raw pixel noise.
Transfer learning is the reuse of a model trained on one task or dataset to improve learning on a related task.
Pretrained visual features are commonly adapted to new tasks with limited data.
Transfer can fail when the source and target domains differ substantially.
Transfer learning is an accommodation mechanism (FIO-02): it adapts a system's focus to a new operating range.
Out-of-distribution detection is the task of identifying inputs that differ from a model's training distribution.
Models can produce confident but wrong predictions on out-of-distribution inputs.
Detecting such inputs is important for safe deployment of machine vision systems.
Out-of-distribution detection is a provenance check (FIO-04): it asks whether an input belongs to the world the model was trained on.
Visual tracking is the machine vision task of following the position of one or more targets across video frames.
Trackers must handle appearance change, occlusion, and motion of the target.
Errors accumulate when a target is lost and then re-acquired incorrectly.
Visual tracking is a suspension over time (FIO-01): target identity and position are held across frames.
Data augmentation is the practice of expanding training data by applying label-preserving transformations to existing images.
Common transformations include cropping, flipping, rotation, and color changes.
Augmentation can improve a model's robustness to variation in its inputs.
Data augmentation is an accommodation strategy (FIO-02): it widens the range over which a model holds focus.
Image registration is the process of aligning two or more images into a common spatial coordinate frame.
It is used to combine images taken at different times, from different viewpoints, or by different sensors.
Registration accuracy is critical in applications such as medical imaging and remote sensing.
Image registration is a suspension of spatial reference (FIO-01): it holds a consistent frame across images.
Scene graph generation is the machine vision task of producing a structured graph of objects and their relationships in an image.
Nodes represent objects and edges represent the relationships between them.
Scene graphs support higher-level reasoning such as visual question answering.
Scene graph generation is an interpretation layer (FIO-05): structured meaning is assembled from detected parts.
A receptive field is the region of sensory space within which a stimulus activates a given sensory neuron.
Ganglion cells, LGN neurons, and cortical cells each have characteristic receptive field sizes and response properties that define their sensitivity to contrast and edges.
Center-surround organization in retinal ganglion cells creates spatial antagonism that enhances edges and suppresses uniform illumination.
Receptive fields are the signal boundaries of the visual system (FIO-03): they define what can be distinguished from what and where the signal-noise threshold is set.
Dark adaptation is the process by which the visual system increases its sensitivity after exposure to bright conditions, driven by photoreceptor recovery.
The process occurs in two phases: an initial rapid cone-mediated phase followed by a slower rod-mediated phase that achieves full sensitivity recovery.
Recovery of rod pigment rhodopsin, bleached by bright light, underlies the slow phase and determines its time course.
Dark adaptation is an accommodation mechanism (FIO-02): the optical system dynamically extends its operating range to recover signal in low-luminance environments.
Light adaptation is the process by which the visual system reduces its sensitivity and adjusts its operating range in response to increased illumination.
Multiple mechanisms contribute, including pupillary constriction, photoreceptor pigment bleaching, and neural gain control.
Light adaptation allows the visual system to operate across a range of light intensities exceeding a factor of a billion.
Light adaptation is an accommodation mechanism (FIO-02): it recalibrates the system's input range across luminance environments without changing optical geometry.
Stereopsis is the perception of depth produced by the visual system's comparison of the slightly different images received by each eye.
The horizontal disparity between corresponding retinal images is the primary binocular cue to relative depth and is processed by specialized neurons in visual cortex.
Stereopsis requires precise binocular alignment; misalignment such as strabismus disrupts the disparity signal and can prevent its development.
Stereopsis is an interpretation (FIO-05): the third dimension of space is not given in the optical input but constructed by the visual system from a binocular difference signal.
Color constancy is the ability of the visual system to perceive the surface color of objects as stable despite changes in the spectral composition of the illuminating light.
The visual system achieves color constancy through comparisons across the visual field and by estimating and discounting the illuminant.
Color constancy is not perfect; under extreme spectral shifts the same surface can appear to have different colors, as illustrated by well-known optical illusions.

Color constancy is an interpretation (FIO-05): perceived surface color is a stable output constructed by the visual system rather than a direct read-out of the wavelength distribution reaching the eye.
The visual field is the spatial extent of the environment that can be perceived when fixation is held steady, bounded by the anatomy of the retina and optic pathways.
The central visual field is processed by the fovea and has the highest spatial resolution; peripheral regions are progressively less resolved.
Defects in the visual field correspond to lesions at specific points along the visual pathway, making field mapping a tool for localizing pathology.
The visual field is a provenance boundary (FIO-04): it defines the scope of what the system can receive, and any region outside it is an unexamined source rather than a confirmed absence.
Vergence refers to the disjunctive movements of the two eyes that maintain alignment of the visual axes on objects at different distances.
Convergence and divergence are driven by disparity signals from corresponding retinal points and by accommodative demand.
Vergence and accommodation are neurally coupled: the vergence-accommodation reflex links the focusing effort of the lens to the binocular alignment of the eyes.
Vergence is an accommodation mechanism (FIO-02): it maintains the geometric alignment required for stereopsis and binocular single vision as viewing distance changes.
Predictive coding is a framework in which the brain continuously generates predictions about sensory input and propagates only the residual error signals not explained by the current model.
According to this framework, perception is driven primarily by top-down predictions rather than bottom-up signals, with sensory signals serving mainly to correct prediction errors.
The framework accounts for a wide range of perceptual phenomena including illusions, perceptual completion, and the efficiency of neural coding.
Predictive coding frames perception as interpretation (FIO-05): the perceived world is not a read-out of sensory input but the brain's best current model, revised at the margin by incoming signal.
Face perception is the visual processing of facial information, including identity, emotional expression, gaze direction, and age, supported by dedicated neural mechanisms.
The fusiform face area and related regions respond preferentially to faces; face processing is holistic and relies on the configuration of internal features relative to the face outline.
Faces are the visual category most resistant to recognition failure and most susceptible to systematic perceptual illusions, including the hollow-face illusion.
Face perception is a high-priority interpretation system (FIO-05): the visual system reads identity, intent, and state from a two-dimensional signal — outputs that are absent from the signal itself.
Object recognition is the visual process by which the identity of an object is determined from its projected two-dimensional image.
The visual system achieves recognition despite large variations in viewpoint, scale, illumination, and occlusion — a computational challenge that occupied decades of research.
Recognition involves a hierarchy of processing stages from oriented edges through intermediate features to object-selective responses in the inferotemporal cortex.
Object recognition is an interpretation (FIO-05): the category assigned to a visual input is a stable judgment reached by the visual system across an enormous range of viewing conditions.
Scene recognition is the process by which the visual system assigns a global meaning to a visual environment from its spatial layout and statistical regularities.
Scene category can be extracted in under 100 milliseconds, before individual objects are identified, from the spatial frequency envelope and global composition of the image.
The gist of a scene is represented before local detail is resolved, indicating that interpretation is not purely bottom-up.
Scene recognition is an interpretation (FIO-05): the meaning of a scene is assigned by the visual system to the global signal before the local signal is fully analyzed.
Visual crowding is the impaired recognition of peripheral targets caused by the presence of nearby flanking elements.
Crowding is a fundamental limit on peripheral vision: the critical spacing between target and flanker scales with eccentricity (Bouma's law).
Crowding cannot be overcome by attention or practice and reflects a hard architectural constraint on how the visual system processes peripheral signals.
Visual crowding is a signal-noise failure (FIO-03): flankers degrade the target signal in the peripheral channel in a way that cannot be resolved by higher-level interpretation.
In machine learning, an attention mechanism is a learned weighting function that selects the most relevant parts of an input representation before producing an output.
Self-attention, the core operation of the transformer architecture, computes pairwise relevance scores between all positions in a sequence and weights their representations accordingly.
Attention mechanisms allow models to selectively focus on task-relevant input regions without requiring that relevance be explicitly specified.

The attention mechanism is the artificial suspension layer of modern ML systems (FIO-01): it holds the relevant parts of the input in context, the machine equivalent of the tension that keeps focus from drifting.
The transformer is a neural network architecture that processes input sequences by stacking self-attention layers with feed-forward networks, replacing recurrence and convolution.
Transformers scale effectively with data and compute; their pre-trained representations transfer across vision and language tasks, underpinning the current generation of large models.
Vision transformers treat images as sequences of patches, applying self-attention to spatial visual inputs.

The transformer architecture is a structural suspension mechanism (FIO-01): it holds input context together through learned attention weights, making stable, position-aware feature integration possible.
A convolutional neural network is a neural network that uses learned, spatially-shared filters to detect local patterns at multiple scales.
The convolutional operation implements a local receptive field: each filter responds to a small spatial region of the input, analogous to the receptive fields of biological visual neurons.
Successive pooling operations make the representation progressively invariant to small translations, enabling recognition despite minor input shifts.
CNNs implement a structural suspension layer (FIO-01): local receptive fields and learned filters hold spatial structure stable across small transformations.
Few-shot learning is the ability of a model to recognize new categories or tasks from a small number of examples after pre-training on a large corpus.
Few-shot learning methods include meta-learning, prototypical networks, and fine-tuning large pre-trained models on minimal labeled data.
The performance gap between human and machine few-shot learning reflects a fundamental difference in prior structure.
Few-shot learning is an accommodation problem (FIO-02): the system must extend its operating range to new categories from minimal signal without destabilizing what it already knows.
A generative model is a statistical model that learns the joint distribution of inputs and can produce new samples indistinguishable from training data.
Generative adversarial networks, variational autoencoders, and diffusion models are the primary architectures; diffusion models dominate recent image synthesis benchmarks.
The near-perfect visual fidelity of outputs from modern generative models severs the assumed link between image appearance and image origin.
Generative models are a provenance failure at scale (FIO-04): they produce appearance without origin, making the appearance-provenance link impossible to assume.
Photopic vision is the visual function of the eye under well-lit conditions, mediated by cone photoreceptors.
Cones are concentrated in and around the fovea; the three cone types (S, M, L) underlie trichromatic color vision.
Photopic vision has high spatial acuity and color discrimination but lower sensitivity than scotopic vision.
Photopic vision is the high-signal operating mode of the visual system (FIO-03): it maximizes signal resolution and color discrimination at the cost of absolute sensitivity.
Scotopic vision is the visual function of the eye under dim light conditions, mediated by rod photoreceptors.
Rods are distributed throughout the peripheral retina, are absent from the fovea, and are roughly 1000 times more sensitive to light than cones.
Scotopic vision lacks color discrimination and has lower spatial resolution than photopic vision.
Scotopic vision operates at the lower signal boundary of the visual system (FIO-03): it extends dynamic range at the cost of signal specificity, trading resolution for detection.
Nystagmus is an involuntary, rhythmic oscillation of the eyes that impairs fixation stability and is the most common oculomotor disorder.
It may be congenital or acquired; acquired nystagmus commonly results from lesions in the brainstem, cerebellum, or vestibular pathways.
The slow drift is the failure of gaze stabilization; the fast corrective saccade is the compensatory reflex.
Nystagmus is an accommodation failure (FIO-02): the oculomotor system fails to maintain stable gaze, and the fixation point drifts continuously rather than holding.
A scotoma is a region of absent or diminished vision within the visual field surrounded by areas of normal or relatively normal vision.
Scotomas arise from damage to retinal tissue, the optic nerve, or higher visual pathways; their shape and location map to the anatomical site of the lesion.
The physiological scotoma at the optic disc is not perceived because the visual system fills it in using surrounding information — the same process as the blind spot.
A scotoma is a provenance gap (FIO-04): the visual system receives no signal from a region of the world yet may not register its own absence, making the gap invisible as a gap.
The accommodation reflex is the coordinated response of the visual system to bring a near object into focus, comprising lens accommodation, pupillary constriction, and convergence.
The three components of the near triad — accommodation, miosis, and convergence — are neurally linked and activated together by the Edinger-Westphal nucleus.
Age-related hardening of the crystalline lens reduces accommodative amplitude, producing presbyopia — the progressive loss of near-focusing range.
The accommodation reflex is a coordinated accommodation mechanism (FIO-02): it reconfigures the optical system to extend its sharp-focus range to near distances on demand.
Active inference is the framework in which organisms minimize surprise by both updating internal models (perception) and acting on the environment to match predictions.
Proposed by Karl Friston, the framework unifies perception, action, and learning under a single objective: the minimization of free energy or surprisal.
Active inference generalizes predictive coding by including action as a mechanism for resolving prediction error, not merely updating beliefs.

Active inference frames all perception as interpretation (FIO-05): the organism does not passively receive the world but actively constructs it, and its actions are determined by its generative model.
Mental imagery is the experience of visual representations in the absence of corresponding external stimulation.
Mental imagery activates overlapping cortical regions with direct visual perception, including early visual areas, though with lower fidelity and resolution.
The ability to generate and manipulate internal visual representations varies substantially across individuals, from vivid imagery to near-absence (aphantasia).
Mental imagery is an interpretation of the absence of signal (FIO-05): the visual system constructs a representation from internal knowledge rather than incoming sensory data.
Multisensory integration is the process by which the brain combines signals from different sensory modalities into a unified percept.
Integration follows the principle of maximum likelihood estimation: signals are combined in proportion to their reliability, with more reliable signals given more weight.
Conflicts between modalities are resolved in favor of whichever modality has the highest reliability for that task.
Multisensory integration is an interpretation (FIO-05): the unified percept of an event is a weighted construction from multiple incomplete signals, not a read-out from any single sensory stream.
Spatial cognition is the set of mental processes by which the brain represents, navigates, and reasons about spatial relationships and locations in the environment.
Hippocampal place cells and entorhinal grid cells form a neural substrate for allocentric spatial representation — a map of the environment independent of the observer's current position.
Spatial memory is constructed incrementally through experience and can be severely disrupted by hippocampal damage while leaving other forms of memory intact.
Spatial cognition is an interpretation (FIO-05): the brain's map of space is a constructed model maintained by inference rather than a direct read-out of environmental structure.
Perceptual grouping is the visual system's tendency to organize elements of the visual scene into coherent units based on proximity, similarity, continuity, and common fate.
The Gestalt principles describe the rules of grouping: elements that are close, similar, or moving together are perceived as belonging to the same object or group.
Grouping is a pre-attentive process that occurs before focused attention and across the entire visual field simultaneously.
Perceptual grouping is a signal-noise operation (FIO-03): it partitions the incoming signal into figure and ground, separating what belongs to a coherent object from what does not.
Temporal integration is the process by which the visual system combines stimuli presented within a brief time window into a single perceptual event.
The temporal resolution of the visual system is limited: stimuli within roughly 50-100 ms of each other may be integrated rather than resolved as separate.
Temporal integration underlies the perception of continuity in flickering light and the inability to resolve rapid sequential events as distinct.
Temporal integration is an accommodation mechanism (FIO-02): it extends the operating range of the visual system across time, treating nearby events as continuous when the gap falls below threshold.
Metacognition is the capacity to monitor and evaluate one's own cognitive processes, including assessing the confidence and accuracy of one's own perceptual judgments.
Perceptual metacognition is separable from perceptual sensitivity: observers can be accurate in their judgments but poorly calibrated in their confidence, or vice versa.
Poor metacognitive accuracy has been linked to deficits in prefrontal function, demonstrating a dissociation between first-order perception and its monitoring.

Metacognition is an interpretation of one's own interpretations (FIO-05): the confidence signal that guides behavior is itself a constructed output that can fail independently of the primary perceptual judgment.
Self-supervised learning is a training paradigm in which a model learns representations by predicting parts of the input from other parts, without human-labeled targets.
Common objectives include masked autoencoding (predicting masked image patches from visible ones) and contrastive learning (aligning representations of augmented views of the same image).
Self-supervised pre-training on large unlabeled datasets produces representations that transfer effectively to downstream tasks with minimal fine-tuning.

Self-supervised learning builds the suspension structure of the model (FIO-01): by learning to predict structure from incomplete signal, the model develops representations that hold stable across deployment variation.
Contrastive learning is a self-supervised training objective that pulls representations of similar inputs together and pushes representations of dissimilar inputs apart.
Positive pairs (different views of the same image) are brought close in representation space; negative pairs are pushed apart, encouraging representations that capture class-relevant signal.
Large batch sizes are required because informative hard negatives — which share some features with the positive — are rare and their frequency grows with batch size.

Contrastive learning is a signal-noise operation (FIO-03): it trains representations to separate signal that identifies an instance from the noise of augmentation, viewpoint, and irrelevant variation.
Zero-shot learning is the ability of a model to correctly classify or describe inputs from categories it has never seen during training, using semantic descriptions or attribute embeddings.
Vision-language models achieve zero-shot classification by matching image embeddings against text embeddings of category names without category-specific training examples.
Zero-shot performance can degrade significantly on fine-grained distinctions or distribution shifts not covered by semantic descriptions used at training time.

Zero-shot learning is an interpretation problem (FIO-05): the model must assign a category to an input using only structural knowledge from training, with no direct signal from examples of the target category.
Video understanding encompasses the machine vision tasks of recognizing actions, tracking objects, and modeling temporal relationships across video frame sequences.
The temporal dimension introduces challenges of motion estimation, identity maintenance across occlusion, and detection of events defined by change rather than appearance.
Video transformers model temporal attention across frames, learning which patches in which frames are relevant to the task.
Video understanding requires temporal suspension (FIO-01): the system must hold the identity of objects and events stable across time, the machine equivalent of maintaining fixation through a scene.
Action recognition is the task of identifying the action being performed in a video or sequence of images based on the temporal pattern of motion and appearance.
Modern systems use spatiotemporal convolutions or video transformers that process temporal patterns alongside spatial appearance features.
Action recognition requires distinguishing intentional from coincidental motion and is sensitive to viewpoint, speed variation, and occlusion of key body parts.
Action recognition is an interpretation of motion (FIO-05): the category (the action) is assigned to a temporal signal based on learned expectation about what motion patterns mean.
The optokinetic reflex is a reflexive eye movement that stabilizes the visual image during sustained whole-field motion by rotating the eyes in the direction of motion.
The reflex is driven by the direction of global visual motion and is mediated by subcortical pathways distinct from those driving voluntary smooth pursuit.
Like the vestibulo-ocular reflex, it operates with a slow phase in the direction of motion and a fast corrective saccade in the opposite direction.
The optokinetic reflex is a gaze suspension mechanism (FIO-02): it holds the image stable on the retina during environmental motion, preventing global retinal slip from destroying acuity.
The vestibulo-ocular reflex stabilizes gaze during head movement by producing compensatory eye movements in the opposite direction to head rotation.
The reflex arc connects the semicircular canals of the inner ear to the extraocular muscles via the brainstem, operating with extremely short latency of approximately 10 milliseconds.
The VOR allows clear vision during locomotion and rapid head movement, and its gain can be adaptively recalibrated through experience.

The vestibulo-ocular reflex is a gaze suspension mechanism (FIO-02): it counteracts head rotation to keep the retinal image stable — the neurological thread that holds focus in place during body movement.
Spatial frequency describes the periodicity of luminance variation across space, measured in cycles per degree of visual angle, and is a fundamental description of the visual signal.
The visual system analyzes the image through channels tuned to different spatial frequencies: low frequencies carry coarse shape and layout; high frequencies carry fine detail and edges.
The contrast sensitivity function describes the visual system's sensitivity at each spatial frequency, peaking at intermediate frequencies and falling off at high and low extremes.
Spatial frequency is a signal decomposition axis (FIO-03): the visual system reads the world as a multi-channel signal in which information at different scales is separated before being integrated.
The contrast threshold is the minimum difference in luminance between a target and its background that is reliably detectable by the visual system.
Threshold contrast depends on spatial frequency, background luminance, stimulus duration, and eccentricity; it is the fundamental limit of signal detection in vision.
Clinical measurement of contrast sensitivity is more sensitive to early retinal and neural pathology than measurement of high-contrast acuity alone.
The contrast threshold is the signal-noise boundary of the visual system (FIO-03): it defines the minimum signal-to-noise ratio required for a stimulus to be resolved.
Visual resolution is the ability of the eye to distinguish fine detail, measured as the minimum angular separation of two points perceived as distinct.
Maximum visual resolution occurs at the fovea, where cone density is highest; resolution falls steeply with eccentricity.
Clinical visual acuity (Snellen chart) measures high-contrast resolution and may understate the impact of conditions that primarily reduce contrast sensitivity.
Visual resolution is a signal discrimination limit (FIO-03): it defines the spatial grain of the signal channel and determines what spatial information can enter the system at all.

Bottom-up processing refers to perceptual analysis driven by the properties of the sensory input itself, proceeding from local features to global interpretation without prior knowledge guiding the analysis.
Low-level features such as edges, orientations, and color contrasts are extracted in early visual cortex before categorical knowledge is applied.
Bottom-up processing is captured in computational models of saliency, which predict which regions attract attention based on local feature contrast alone.
Bottom-up processing is a signal-reading stage (FIO-03): it extracts the features of the signal without prediction, providing the raw data on which top-down interpretation subsequently operates.
Cognitive load is the total amount of mental effort being used in working memory at a given time, limiting the capacity available for perception and task performance.
Working memory capacity is limited to roughly four chunks of information simultaneously; tasks that exceed this limit cause performance degradation and errors.
High cognitive load reduces perceptual sensitivity, increasing inattentional blindness rates and narrowing the focus of attention.

Cognitive load is an accommodation constraint (FIO-02): it defines the operating envelope of the perceptual system under demand, narrowing the range of what can be held in focus when resources are saturated.
The attentional blink is the temporary impairment in detecting a second target that occurs when it follows a first target within roughly 200-500 ms in a rapid serial visual presentation.
The phenomenon is attributed to the consolidation of the first target into working memory, which temporarily depletes attentional resources for subsequent items.
The attentional blink can be reduced or eliminated if the second target immediately follows the first (Lag-1 sparing effect) or if task demands are reduced.
The attentional blink is an accommodation failure (FIO-02): the system temporarily cannot extend its processing range to a second target, creating a temporal gap in the perceptual record.
Perceptual adaptation is the process by which the visual system recalibrates its sensitivity or interpretation framework following prolonged exposure to a particular stimulus.

After prolonged exposure to a tilted grating, a vertical grating appears tilted in the opposite direction — the tilt after-effect — demonstrating that adaptation alters the perceptual reference for orientation.
Adaptation operates at multiple levels of the visual system, from early photoreceptor adaptation to high-level categorical after-effects on face and object perception.

Perceptual adaptation is a recalibration of interpretation (FIO-05): the visual system adjusts the reference frame it uses to assign meaning to incoming signals, making the same signal produce a different output after exposure.
Visual attention is the selective processing of part of the visual scene at the expense of other parts, determining what receives deeper analysis and reaches awareness.
Attention can be directed by stimulus salience (bottom-up) or by goals and expectations (top-down); the relative influence depends on task demands and sensory context.
Both spatial attention and feature-based attention modulate activity in early visual cortex, amplifying signals from attended locations or features.

Visual attention is the suspension mechanism of perception (FIO-01): it holds selected parts of the visual scene in the state required for detailed analysis — the cognitive analogue of zonular tension.
Spatial attention is the selective enhancement of processing for stimuli at a particular location in the visual field, operating like a spotlight that brightens one region of the scene.
Attending to a location increases the apparent contrast of stimuli there, speeds detection responses, and reduces the contrast threshold for target detection.
Spatial attention can be covertly directed to locations other than the point of fixation, demonstrating that the locus of attention and the locus of gaze are distinct.
Spatial attention is a suspension mechanism (FIO-01): it holds a selected region of the visual scene in enhanced processing, maintaining the focus of analysis independent of the direction of gaze.
Feature-based attention is the selective enhancement of processing for a particular visual feature value — a color, orientation, or motion direction — across the entire visual field simultaneously.
Attention to a feature value such as red enhances processing of all red items in the scene regardless of location and suppresses processing of opposite feature values.
Feature-based attention operates globally and simultaneously across the visual field, in contrast to spatial attention, which is local and sequential.

Feature-based attention is a signal selection mechanism (FIO-03): it amplifies signal at a target feature value while suppressing flanking values, reducing the signal-noise ratio for that feature across the scene.
Image quality assessment is the task of measuring or predicting the perceptual quality of an image, including degradations from noise, blur, compression, and distortion.
No-reference (blind) image quality metrics predict quality without access to a pristine reference image, using learned statistical regularities of natural images.
Full-reference metrics such as SSIM compare a degraded image to an undegraded reference across luminance, contrast, and structure channels.

Image quality assessment is a signal-noise measurement (FIO-03): it quantifies how far the signal channel has degraded from the intact signal, the machine analogue of contrast sensitivity measurement in biological vision.
Visual question answering is the task of producing a correct textual answer to a natural language question about the content of an image.
VQA requires the integration of visual recognition, spatial reasoning, common-sense knowledge, and language understanding; state-of-the-art systems use vision-language transformers.
Accuracy on standard VQA benchmarks can mask failure on out-of-distribution questions, adversarial phrasings, or questions requiring compositional reasoning.

Visual question answering is an interpretation task (FIO-05): the system must assign a meaning to a visual input conditioned on a linguistic query — a double interpretation that can fail at the visual, the linguistic, or the compositional level.
Instance segmentation is the task of simultaneously detecting, classifying, and producing pixel-level masks for each individual instance of each object class in an image.
Instance segmentation combines the challenges of object detection (where?) and semantic segmentation (what shape?), distinguishing individual instances of the same class.
Transformer-based methods such as Mask2Former attend to object queries and achieve state-of-the-art accuracy across diverse object categories.

Instance segmentation is an interpretation task (FIO-05): the system must not only label each pixel but assign each pixel to a specific individual object — a judgment that goes beyond the input signal.
Gaze estimation is the task of determining the direction in which a person is looking from an image or video of their face or eyes.
Appearance-based gaze estimation uses deep learning to map from the cropped eye region or full face to gaze direction, without requiring calibration hardware.
Gaze estimation from monocular cameras remains challenging under varying head pose, illumination, and resolution, and errors increase with distance from the camera.
Gaze estimation is a structural tracking problem (FIO-01): the system must maintain a stable, accurate model of where attention is directed by tracking the oculomotor system of the observed person.
Image synthesis is the computational generation of new images from noise, text descriptions, or partial inputs using learned statistical models.
Diffusion models, generative adversarial networks, and autoregressive models are the primary synthesis architectures; diffusion models dominate recent image synthesis benchmarks.
Synthesized images are increasingly indistinguishable from photographs under direct visual inspection, requiring forensic analysis or provenance metadata to verify origin.
Image synthesis inverts the provenance chain (FIO-04): it produces appearance without an originating scene, creating the class of inputs for which provenance cannot be assumed.
Weakness, stretching, or rupture of the zonular fibers can allow the lens to shift from its normal centered position, a displacement described clinically as ectopia lentis.
Zonular failure is the prototype of FIO-01: the focal element remains intact while the structure that holds it gives way, so focus is lost without the lens itself being damaged.
Presbyopia, the progressive age-related loss of accommodative amplitude, typically becomes noticeable in the fifth decade of life as the crystalline lens loses elasticity.
Presbyopia is the clearest everyday instance of FIO-02: the suspension is intact and the eye still focuses, but the range across which it can hold focus has narrowed.
